title: Lost in Transition: The Dark Side of Emerging Adulthood By Christian Smith, with Kari Christoffersen, Hilary Davidson and Patricia Snell Herzog Oxford University Press. 2011. 296 pages. $27.95 cloth
abstract: As in the case of debates about educational systems, everyone considers himself or herself an expert on youth issues. After all, everyone had experiences with this period of life, just as everyone has experiences with educational systems. Personal experiences qualify one as an expert, right? Well, it depends how you define “expert.” If we define the expert as someone with a comprehensive and authoritative knowledge of a specific area, very few people qualify as experts in the sociology of youth, including the public and the author of this book. Christian Smith wrote this book with material left over from a study whose results were published in 2009 in a book with an almost identical title, Souls in Transition (226). That book focused on the religious beliefs of this same sample. However, Smith tells us that the current book tells more of a story beyond the religious lives of his respondents. This story is that a sizable portion of today's young people is morally adrift, indulgent with drugs and alcohol, materialistic hyperconsumers, sex obsessed and politically apathetic.
